summary: Drew DeVault announces the 1.0 release of sway, an i3-compatible Wayland compositor for Linux and FreeBSD, after 1,315 days of development. The release required nearly rewriting the entire Linux desktop from scratch, with contributions from almost 300 people across 9,000 commits. A key outcome was the creation of wlroots, a shared library that has become foundational to the broader Wayland ecosystem. DeVault highlights the ecosystem of interoperable tools built on Wayland standards and outlines future plans including accessibility, internationalization, and mobile support.
key_points: Sway 1.0 is a complete overhaul from 0.15, representing a stable, flexible Wayland desktop built by ~300 contributors over 9,000 commits
The wlroots library was created to provide a common base for Wayland compositors and has been adopted by dozens of projects across the ecosystem
Sway serves as a political force in the Wayland ecosystem, pushing for standardization and cooperation among projects
A broad ecosystem of interoperable Wayland tools has emerged alongside sway, from screen lockers to clipboard managers
Future plans include accessibility improvements, CJK input methods, mobile device support, and sandboxing features
money_quotes: In order to complete this project, we have had to rewrite the entire Linux desktop nearly from scratch.
Nearly 300 people worked together, together writing over 9,000 commits and almost 100,000 lines of code, to bring you this release.
0.15 was a buggy, frustrating desktop to use, but sway 1.0 has been completely overhauled and represents a much more capable desktop.
Sway 1.0 exists as a positive political force in the Wayland ecosystem pushing for standardization and cooperation among Wayland projects.
We realized that the Wayland ecosystem was sorely in need of a stable & flexible common base library to encapsulate all of the difficult and complex facets of building a desktop.
As with all sway features, we intend to have the best-in-class implementations of these features and set the bar as high as we can for everyone else.
key_insight: Building sway required not just a window manager but an entire shared infrastructure layer (wlroots) that elevated the whole Wayland ecosystem, demonstrating how ambitious free software projects create value far beyond their original scope.
